draft.introduction: Job 42 concludes the book with Job’s response to God’s speeches from the whirlwind. Having encountered God directly, Job moves from hearsay knowledge to experiential knowledge: “now my eye sees you.” From the Septuagint.

translation.text: Then Job answered the Lord and said: “I know that you can do all things, and that no purpose of yours can be thwarted. Who is this that hides counsel without knowledge? Therefore I have uttered what I did not understand, things too wonderful for me, which I did not know. I had heard of you by the hearing of the ear, but now my eye sees you; therefore I despise myself, and repent in dust and ashes.”
translation.original_text: ὑπολαβὼν δὲ Ιωβ λέγει τῷ κυρίῳ

translator_notes.note: The contrast between hearing and seeing—“I had heard of you, but now my eye sees you”—marks the transition from secondhand theology to direct encounter. Job’s questions about suffering are never answered intellectually; they are dissolved by the overwhelming experience of divine presence. The theosis tradition would call this transformative vision theoria.
